pairings.note: Rosaceae astringents rich in hydrolysable tannins, traditionally used for diarrhoea and as gargles; studied together for antioxidant and genotoxicity profile.
Rosaceae astringents rich in hydrolysable tannins, traditionally used together for diarrhoea, sore throat and as gargles.
Ashwagandha and kava both calm the central nervous system; combining them may add to sedation and drowsiness, so combined use warrants caution.
Both ashwagandha and valerian are calming, sleep-promoting herbs; using them together may add to drowsiness and sedation, so take care especially before driving or when using other sedatives.
Both are traditional diuretic 'flushing' herbs for irrigation therapy of the urinary tract.

Fenugreek and bitter melon can each lower blood sugar, so taking them together — especially alongside diabetes medicines — may add up and increase the risk of hypoglycaemia. Monitor your blood glucose.
Gymnema and bitter melon both lower blood sugar; combined use may add up and risk hypoglycaemia, particularly with diabetes drugs, so monitor blood glucose.

Cinnamon and bitter melon can each lower blood sugar, so taking them together — especially alongside diabetes medicines — may add up and increase the risk of hypoglycaemia. Monitor your blood glucose.

Fenugreek and black seed (Nigella sativa) can each lower blood sugar, so taking them together — especially alongside diabetes medicines — may add up and increase the risk of hypoglycaemia. Monitor your blood glucose.

Garlic and black seed (Nigella sativa) can each mildly lower blood pressure, so taking them together — especially alongside blood-pressure medicines — may add up and lower blood pressure more than expected. Monitor your blood pressure.

Hibiscus and black seed (Nigella sativa) can each mildly lower blood pressure, so taking them together — especially alongside blood-pressure medicines — may add up and lower blood pressure more than expected. Monitor your blood pressure.

Olive leaf and black seed (Nigella sativa) can each mildly lower blood pressure, so taking them together — especially alongside blood-pressure medicines — may add up and lower blood pressure more than expected. Monitor your blood pressure.
Gymnema and cassia cinnamon are both used to lower blood sugar; together their effects may add up, so watch for low blood sugar when combining them or using them with diabetes medication.

Lavender and chamomile are both calming herbs used to ease anxiety and aid sleep; taken together their sedative effects can add up, so while they may help relaxation they can also increase drowsiness — avoid combining with sedative medicines or before driving.
Chamomile and lemon balm are both gentle calming herbs commonly combined for stress and sleep; used together their mild sedative effects may add up.
Chamomile and passionflower are both gentle calming, sleep-supporting herbs traditionally combined; used together they may reinforce each other's soothing, sleep-promoting effect.
Chamomile and valerian are both traditional mild sedatives for restlessness and poor sleep; taken together they may enhance calming and sleep effects but also add to drowsiness.
Classic diuretic pairing for fluid retention and urinary support; both increase urine flow.
Rosaceae astringents rich in hydrolysable tannins, traditionally used for diarrhoea and as gargles; studied together for antioxidant and genotoxicity profile.

Sibling species: both are salicylate- and tannin-rich Rosaceae with overlapping anti-inflammatory, analgesic, astringent and gastroprotective uses (dropwort of dry grassland, meadowsweet of wet meadows); studied together in the same trials.
Both are salicylate-containing anti-inflammatory and analgesic herbs (the classic plant-aspirin group), traditionally combined for rheumatic pain and fever.
Aromatic Apiaceae seeds traditionally paired as carminative and diuretic digestive teas.

Fenugreek and bitter melon can each lower blood sugar, so taking them together — especially alongside diabetes medicines — may add up and increase the risk of hypoglycaemia. Monitor your blood glucose.

Fenugreek and black seed (Nigella sativa) can each lower blood sugar, so taking them together — especially alongside diabetes medicines — may add up and increase the risk of hypoglycaemia. Monitor your blood glucose.
Gymnema and fenugreek both lower blood sugar; taking them together may add up and push blood glucose too low, especially alongside diabetes medication, so monitor and use caution.

Cinnamon and fenugreek can each lower blood sugar, so taking them together — especially alongside diabetes medicines — may add up and increase the risk of blood sugar dropping too low (hypoglycaemia). Monitor your blood glucose.

Garlic and feverfew both reduce blood clotting/platelet stickiness, so taking them together may add up and raise the risk of bruising or bleeding — use caution, especially before surgery or alongside blood-thinning drugs.

Feverfew and ginger both reduce blood clotting/platelet stickiness, so taking them together may add up and raise the risk of bruising or bleeding — use caution, especially before surgery or alongside blood-thinning drugs.

Ginkgo and feverfew both reduce blood clotting/platelet stickiness, so taking them together may add up and raise the risk of bruising or bleeding — use caution, especially before surgery or alongside blood-thinning drugs.

Ginseng and feverfew both reduce blood clotting/platelet stickiness, so taking them together may add up and raise the risk of bruising or bleeding — use caution, especially before surgery or alongside blood-thinning drugs.
Both are traditional diuretic 'flushing' herbs for irrigation therapy of the urinary tract.
Classic urinary irrigation-therapy pairing: both are aquaretic diuretics used to increase urine flow in cystitis and urinary gravel.
Both are Fabaceae rich in the isoflavone phytoestrogen formononetin; combined use adds to phytoestrogen exposure - use caution in hormone-sensitive conditions.

Gymnema and cassia cinnamon are both used to lower blood sugar; together their effects may add up, so watch for low blood sugar when combining them or using them with diabetes medication.

Hibiscus and black seed (Nigella sativa) can each mildly lower blood pressure, so taking them together — especially alongside blood-pressure medicines — may add up and lower blood pressure more than expected. Monitor your blood pressure.

Hops and lemon balm are both mild sedative, sleep-supporting herbs often combined in calming blends; used together they may reinforce each other's relaxing effect.
Hops and passionflower are both GABA-acting sedative herbs commonly combined in sleep blends; together they may enhance relaxation and additive drowsiness.

Valerian and hops are traditionally combined for sleep; a controlled trial found the valerian-hops combination shortened the time to fall asleep more than valerian alone. Both add to sedation, so expect stronger drowsiness.
Ashwagandha and kava both calm the central nervous system; combining them may add to sedation and drowsiness, so combined use warrants caution.
Kava and passionflower are both relaxing, sedating herbs; combined use may deepen drowsiness and sedation, so use caution.

St John's wort is a strong enzyme inducer and kava is a liver-metabolised sedative; combining them may add to central-nervous-system effects and alter how kava is processed, so combined use warrants caution.
Kava and valerian are both calming, sedating herbs; taken together they may add up to cause excessive drowsiness, so use caution and avoid before driving or combining with other sedatives.

Lavender and chamomile are both calming herbs used to ease anxiety and aid sleep; taken together their sedative effects can add up, so while they may help relaxation they can also increase drowsiness — avoid combining with sedative medicines or before driving.

Chamomile and lemon balm are both gentle calming herbs commonly combined for stress and sleep; used together their mild sedative effects may add up.
Hops and lemon balm are both mild sedative, sleep-supporting herbs often combined in calming blends; used together they may reinforce each other's relaxing effect.

Lavender and lemon balm are both calming herbs used to ease anxiety and aid sleep; taken together their sedative effects can add up, so while they may help relaxation they can also increase drowsiness — avoid combining with sedative medicines or before driving.
Lemon balm and passionflower are both gentle, GABA-acting calming herbs often blended together in anti-anxiety and sleep formulas; combined use may deepen relaxation but also add to drowsiness.
Valerian and lemon balm are a classic calming pair acting on the GABA system to improve sleep quality; combined use may increase sedation.

Sibling species: both are salicylate- and tannin-rich Rosaceae with overlapping anti-inflammatory, analgesic, astringent and gastroprotective uses (dropwort of dry grassland, meadowsweet of wet meadows); studied together in the same trials.
Both are Rosaceae rich in ellagitannins and phenolics with overlapping antioxidant and anti-inflammatory profiles; water avens is astringent, meadowsweet more salicylate-based.

Olive leaf and black seed (Nigella sativa) can each mildly lower blood pressure, so taking them together — especially alongside blood-pressure medicines — may add up and lower blood pressure more than expected. Monitor your blood pressure.

Ginseng and feverfew both reduce blood clotting/platelet stickiness, so taking them together may add up and raise the risk of bruising or bleeding — use caution, especially before surgery or alongside blood-thinning drugs.

Garlic and ginseng both reduce blood clotting/platelet stickiness, so taking them together may add up and raise the risk of bruising or bleeding — use caution, especially before surgery or alongside blood-thinning drugs.

Both are Apiaceae diuretics traditionally combined for urinary and 'gravel' complaints; complementary diuretic and urinary-support action.
Chamomile and passionflower are both gentle calming, sleep-supporting herbs traditionally combined; used together they may reinforce each other's soothing, sleep-promoting effect.
Hops and passionflower are both GABA-acting sedative herbs commonly combined in sleep blends; together they may enhance relaxation and additive drowsiness.
Kava and passionflower are both relaxing, sedating herbs; combined use may deepen drowsiness and sedation, so use caution.
Lemon balm and passionflower are both gentle, GABA-acting calming herbs often blended together in anti-anxiety and sleep formulas; combined use may deepen relaxation but also add to drowsiness.
Valerian and passionflower are both mild GABA-acting sedatives often blended in sleep and calming formulas; combined use may enhance relaxation but also additive drowsiness.

St John's wort is a strong enzyme inducer and kava is a liver-metabolised sedative; combining them may add to central-nervous-system effects and alter how kava is processed, so combined use warrants caution.

St John's wort is a strong enzyme inducer and increases serotonin activity; combining it with another antidepressant-type herb such as rhodiola may add to serotonergic effects and unpredictably change how each is handled by the body.

St John's wort raises serotonin activity and can cause serotonin syndrome when combined with other serotonin-boosting agents; pairing it with another mood-active, serotonergic herb such as saffron may add to this risk.

St John's wort speeds up the breakdown of many substances and also raises serotonin activity; pairing it with a sedative herb such as valerian may add to drowsiness and can unpredictably change how each is handled by the body.
Both are Fabaceae rich in the isoflavone phytoestrogen formononetin; combined use adds to phytoestrogen exposure - use caution in hormone-sensitive conditions.

St John's wort is a strong enzyme inducer and increases serotonin activity; combining it with another antidepressant-type herb such as rhodiola may add to serotonergic effects and unpredictably change how each is handled by the body.
Rhodiola and schisandra are adaptogens co-formulated in clinically studied combinations for stress, fatigue and mental performance; combined use is intended to be synergistic rather than harmful.

Rhodiola and eleuthero (Siberian ginseng) are classic adaptogens combined in traditional and studied fixed formulas to build resistance to stress and fatigue; used together their stress-protective effects are complementary.

St John's wort raises serotonin activity and can cause serotonin syndrome when combined with other serotonin-boosting agents; pairing it with another mood-active, serotonergic herb such as saffron may add to this risk.
Rhodiola and schisandra are adaptogens co-formulated in clinically studied combinations for stress, fatigue and mental performance; combined use is intended to be synergistic rather than harmful.
Eleuthero and schisandra are adaptogens combined in traditional and clinically studied formulas to improve stamina and resistance to stress and fatigue; combined use is complementary.

Rhodiola and eleuthero (Siberian ginseng) are classic adaptogens combined in traditional and studied fixed formulas to build resistance to stress and fatigue; used together their stress-protective effects are complementary.
Eleuthero and schisandra are adaptogens combined in traditional and clinically studied formulas to improve stamina and resistance to stress and fatigue; combined use is complementary.
Classic Rosaceae astringent pairing: both are tannin-rich roots traditionally combined for diarrhoea and as mouth/throat gargles.
